content.text: Let me eyeball an actual page to judge scan quality and layout.

Bottom line up front: **Yes, very feasible — and your scans are close to the best-case input for it.** The OCR itself is the easy part. The real work is layout analysis (telling text from figures/tables and preserving reading order in the Word doc), plus one hard limit in `.docx` you should know about before deciding how far to push.

What I looked at

- 580 `.jpg` pages across the 5 book folders (98 / 192 / 96 / 89 / 105).
- All uniform 1436×1527 RGB, crisp text with clean tables and even hyperlinks visible.
- Assumption (high confidence): these are rendered ebook/PDF exports, not phone photos of paper. That means no skew, no lighting noise, no page curl — OCR accuracy will be very high with minimal preprocessing.

The one hard constraint

- **Word is a reflowable format, not fixed-layout.** You can preserve reading order, embed each figure/diagram inline where it belongs, and rebuild tables as real Word tables. You cannot pixel-match arbitrary print layout (side-by-side columns, exact figure placement) — that's a PDF capability, not a `.docx` one. If you need exact visual fidelity, searchable-PDF is the right target and Word is the wrong one. Flagging this now because it determines the whole approach.

Three feasible approaches, increasing fidelity and effort

- **Tier 1 — OCR only (Tesseract + python-docx).** Text-only doc. Simple, fast. Drops all images/tables. Does *not* meet your "preserve pictures and diagrams" requirement, so it's out except as a fallback.
- **Tier 2 — Layout-aware pipeline (recommended).** A page-segmentation model classifies each region as text / title / figure / table. Text regions → Tesseract → paragraphs. Figure regions → cropped and embedded as images. Table regions → reconstructed as Word tables. Assembled top-to-bottom in reading order. This is the sweet spot for what you asked.
- **Tier 3 — Vision-LLM per page.** Send each page image to a vision model that emits structured content (markdown/HTML) → convert to docx. Best table/figure-caption handling, least custom code, but 580 API calls, cost, and per-page variance.

The part you specifically asked about — distinguishing pictures/diagrams from text — is a solved category called **document layout analysis / page segmentation**. Viable engines: PaddleOCR **PP-Structure**, **Surya**, or **docTR**. All return typed regions (text/figure/table) with bounding boxes, which is exactly what you need to crop images and preserve them.

Things that will bite (worth deciding up front)

- **Tables** are the hardest element — structure recognition (cell grid) is a separate step from OCR and where most errors land. PP-Structure and Surya both do it, imperfectly.
- **Hyperlinks** (the blue "Chapter 15" text) will OCR as plain text; live links are lost unless specifically reconstructed.
- **Headers/footers/page numbers** will get captured as body text unless you filter them.
- **Reading order** across a page break isn't inferred — each page is independent; you'd concatenate per book.
- **Copyright**: these are commercial titles. Fine for your own format-shifting; just noting the output shouldn't be redistributed.

Fit with your stack: Python throughout, batch-friendly at this volume, and it containerizes cleanly. Per your policy I'd default the container to an openSUSE BCI base — worth noting the ML layout models (Paddle/Torch) pull heavy dependencies, so the image won't be small; I'd justify base-image choice concretely when we get there.

content.text:
That changes the calculus in your favor — searchable PDF is the *right* target for scanned pages, and it's both easier and higher-fidelity than the Word path.

Why it's a better fit

- **The layout problem disappears entirely.** A searchable PDF keeps your original page image untouched and slides an invisible OCR text layer behind it. Figures, diagrams, tables, columns, hyperlink styling — all preserved perfectly, because you're not reconstructing anything. The pixels stay exactly as scanned.
- **You no longer need to distinguish pictures from text.** That was the hard, error-prone part of the docx approach. Here it's moot — the picture stays where it is; OCR only has to find words to make selectable/searchable. Table cell structure being imperfect in the text layer doesn't matter, because the visible table is the untouched image.
- **Effort drops from "medium pipeline" to "basically a two-step batch job."**

The standard tool is **OCRmyPDF** (a wrapper around Tesseract, purpose-built for exactly this)

- Combine each book's JPGs into a PDF losslessly (`img2pdf`), then run `ocrmypdf` to add the text layer. Per book, roughly two commands.
- Output: 5 searchable PDFs, one per book, pages identical to your scans, fully searchable, copy-pasteable, and screen-reader accessible.
- Given your scan quality, the text-layer accuracy will be high with default settings — no deskew/denoise gymnastics needed.

Optional niceties, all cheap to add later

- **Bookmarks / clickable TOC** per book (nice for 90–190 page files).
- **Compression** (`ocrmypdf --optimize`) if file size matters; your pages are ~200–400KB each so a book lands in the tens of MB.
- **Page-count sanity check** as the verification step (input JPGs vs output PDF pages).

Fits your stack cleanly: pure CLI/Python, trivially containerized. Per your policy I'd base the container on openSUSE BCI — `ocrmypdf` and `tesseract` are available there, and I'd confirm the exact package route before building.

Two decisions worth settling before I implement (not now — just flagging):

- One PDF per book (my default) vs. any combined/merged output?
- Add bookmarks/TOC, or keep it plain searchable PDFs for a first pass?

Net: feasible, and switching to searchable PDF makes it simpler *and* better than the Word plan. Want me to write the script when you're ready?

These are going to be ingested into a milvus database for ai consumption, so optional niceties should be selected with that in mind. Separate pdfs per book. Will this preserve tables if they're split over multiple pages?

Direct answer: **No. A searchable PDF will not preserve a table that spans multiple pages — it'll fragment into two disconnected blobs, one per page. And that same limitation applies to nearly every automated route, so it's worth solving deliberately rather than assuming any tool handles it for free.**

Why the searchable-PDF path fails on tables

- OCRmyPDF's text layer is **per-page and purely positional** — it drops each recognized word at the pixel location where it sits on the image. It carries zero table semantics: no cells, no rows, no column model.
- A table split across pages 50–51 becomes two independent positional word-clouds. Nothing links row 8 on page 50 to its continuation on page 51.
- Even within one page, when you later *extract* text from that PDF for chunking, a table comes out as space/newline-separated word soup — often column-jumbled. Visually the PDF looks perfect (the image is untouched), but the text you feed Milvus is degraded exactly where tables are.

The bigger reframe now that the target is Milvus

The searchable PDF is a fine **human-viewable artifact**, but it's a mediocre **ingestion source**. For a vector DB, the real deliverable is clean, chunked text with good structure and provenance. That shifts which "niceties" matter:

- **Page-number metadata per chunk** — high value. Gives you retrieval provenance/citations back to the exact scanned page. This is the nicety I'd prioritize.
- **Section/heading metadata** — chunk on semantic boundaries (headings), keep each table in its own chunk rather than blindly splitting at N tokens.
- **Tables emitted as markdown or HTML** — so structure survives into the chunk and embeds meaningfully.
- **Figures/diagrams** — a raw cropped image is useless to a text embedding. For RAG you want each diagram represented as a short text description/caption (a vision model can generate one), or deliberately skipped. Worth an explicit decision.
- **Bookmarks/TOC/compression** — low value for Milvus; skip for the ingestion pipeline (fine to keep in the human PDF).

Cross-page tables specifically — honest ranking of options

- **Best automatic:** vision-LLM per page emitting clean markdown, plus a merge pass that stitches a trailing headerless table at the bottom of page N onto the table at the top of page N+1. Strongest results, still needs a validation pass.
- **Layout models (PP-Structure/Surya):** detect table regions and structure per page, but cross-page merging is DIY heuristics and brittle.
- **Accept fragmentation:** rely on chunk overlap and the retriever at query time. For many RAG use cases this is genuinely good enough, and these 5 books likely contain only a handful of multi-page tables — a quick manual fix-up may beat any automation.

My assumption (flag): these business/psychology titles are mostly prose with occasional small tables and figures, so multi-page tables are probably rare. If true, the cost of perfect cross-page stitching outweighs the benefit, and targeted manual review of the few offenders is the pragmatic call. I haven't verified this — I scanned two pages, not all 580.

A couple of forks genuinely change the design, so let me pin them down:

questions.question: For the Milvus ingestion source, how should table structure be handled?

options.label: Vision-LLM + merge pass
options.description: Per-page vision model emits markdown tables; a merge step stitches cross-page tables. Best fidelity, highest effort/cost (~580 page calls), needs validation.

options.label: Layout model, per-page
options.description: PP-Structure/Surya extract table structure per page. Good single-page tables, cross-page tables stay fragmented. Local, no API cost.

options.label: Accept fragmentation
options.description: Simplest OCR text; tables chunk as-is and rely on the retriever. Flag the few multi-page tables for manual fix-up.

questions.question: How should figures and diagrams be represented for the vector DB?

options.label: Text descriptions
options.description: A vision model writes a short caption/description of each diagram so it's searchable in the text index. Adds cost per figure.

options.label: Skip figures
options.description: Ignore images entirely in the ingestion text; only real body text and tables get embedded.

options.label: Keep image refs only
options.description: Extract nothing from the image but record a reference/page pointer in chunk metadata so you can retrieve the source page.

questions.question: Which outputs do you want produced?

options.label: Both PDF + chunks
options.description: Searchable PDF per book (human artifact) AND structured chunk file (JSONL/markdown) ready for Milvus ingestion.

options.label: Ingestion chunks only
options.description: Just the structured, chunked text + metadata for Milvus. No human-facing PDF.

options.label: Searchable PDF only
options.description: Only the per-book searchable PDFs; you handle extraction/chunking downstream yourself.

Based on the search results, I found information about Karakeep's supported import formats:

## Karakeep Supported Import Formats

Karakeep (previously Hoarder) is a self-hostable "bookmark everything" service for saving links, notes, images, PDFs, and archived pages in one searchable library.

### Import Format Support

Pocket export gives you an HTML file. Raindrop gives you CSV or HTML. Pinboard gives you JSON. Karakeep handles all of them via the import screen at Settings → Import.

Additional import formats include:

- Chrome and Firefox: export bookmarks as HTML and import them in Karakeep settings.
- Pocket: import the CSV from your export.
- Omnivore: export JSON files, optionally merge with jq, then import.
- Linkwarden: dedicated importer available, handy for migrations.

### Markdown and PDF Support

Regarding markdown files, Karakeep introduced .md file support! A contributor added md. file support! Drop in a PDF, it gets OCR'd and indexed.

### Self-Hosted Capabilities

The REST API and multiple client libraries enable integration into existing workflows, while bulk actions and automatic RSS feed hoarding streamline content ingestion at scale.

content.text:
Answer to your question: **the Milvus chunks are inherently many records, not one document — but that's the wrong artifact to hand Karakeep anyway.** The two systems want different granularities, and the clean design gives each what it wants from one shared intermediate.

How the two layers differ

- **Karakeep is document-level.** Each entry is one item (a PDF, a note, a link, and now a `.md` file). It runs its own OCR + full-text index + AI tagging per item. So "readily obvious what's being sourced" means **one entry per book** → 5 entries total.
- **Milvus is chunk-level.** One book becomes dozens-to-hundreds of small records. You'd store them as a single JSONL *file* per book (many rows), but conceptually it's many vectors, not one document.

Recommended shape (one canonical intermediate feeding both)

- **Per book: one clean Markdown file** — the vision-LLM output with merged tables and inline figure descriptions. This is the canonical text.
  - → Upload to **Karakeep** as the single visible source-of-record entry for that book.
  - → Chunk it into **one JSONL file** for **Milvus**.
- Result: 5 Markdown files, 5 Karakeep entries, 5 JSONL chunk files. Clean 1:1:1 mapping per book.

Why Markdown (not the searchable PDF) is the better thing to feed Karakeep

- Karakeep OCRs PDFs with its own baseline engine. If you feed it the raw/searchable PDF, it re-derives mediocre text and discards the expensive work — merged cross-page tables and figure descriptions get lost.
- Feeding Karakeep the Markdown hands it your *good* text directly, so its full-text search and AI tags operate on the high-quality version. Karakeep's `.md` support (a recent addition) makes this viable.
- Assumption to confirm: your flow is material → Karakeep as the provenance/registry, and separately → Milvus for vectors, with the two linked. If that's right, the searchable PDF becomes optional — nice for a human to eyeball the original page, but not required by either system.

Closing the provenance loop (the part that makes sourcing "obvious")

- Sequence it: build per-book Markdown → upload to Karakeep → capture the Karakeep bookmark ID → stamp that ID (plus book title, page numbers, section heading) into every Milvus chunk's metadata.
- Then any vector hit traces back: chunk → page → book → the exact Karakeep entry. That's the provenance you're after, and Karakeep exposes a REST API to automate the ID capture.

So, revisiting the third fork you left open — my recommendation is **Markdown-per-book + JSONL-chunks-per-book, from one shared extraction**, with the searchable PDF as an optional extra rather than a core output. Two things to confirm before any implementation:

- Do you want the searchable PDF at all, or is Markdown-for-Karakeep + JSONL-for-Milvus sufficient?
- Should the script call the Karakeep API to upload and capture IDs, or do you upload to Karakeep manually and keep that step out of the pipeline?

Sources: [Karakeep (GitHub)](https://github.com/karakeep-app/karakeep), [.md file support · Discussion #1638](https://github.com/karakeep-app/karakeep/discussions/1638)
I just want the markdown for karakeep. I have a pipeline that will ingest the documents from karakeep into milvus, leveraging a specific format.

Scope is now clean, and this is the most feasible version of everything we've discussed. Final read:

- **Input:** 580 JPGs across 5 books (already high-quality, no rescanning).
- **Process:** vision-LLM per page → Markdown, with cross-page tables merged and figure/diagram descriptions written inline.
- **Output:** one Markdown file per book. 5 files. Nothing else.
- Your pipeline owns Karakeep upload and the Karakeep → Milvus step, so no PDF, no JSONL, no chunking, no API work on my side.

The one thing that now drives the Markdown structure

Since your Karakeep → Milvus pipeline "leverages a specific format," the Markdown needs to match whatever that pipeline parses. That's the single remaining dependency. Most likely it cares about some subset of:

- **Page-boundary markers** for provenance — e.g. an HTML comment `<!-- page: 50 -->` or a heading per page. Does your pipeline split/attribute by page?
- **Front matter** — YAML block with title/author/source at the top of each file?
- **Heading hierarchy** — preserve the book's own H1/H2/H3 so your pipeline can chunk on sections?
- **Table format** — GitHub-flavored Markdown tables, or HTML tables?
- **Figure descriptions** — how delimited so they're distinguishable from body text (e.g. `> **Figure:** …` or an image-alt convention)?

If you share the format spec (or a sample document your pipeline already ingests cleanly), I'll match it exactly. Otherwise my default would be: YAML front matter per book, preserved heading hierarchy, `<!-- page: N -->` markers at each page break, GFM tables, and figure descriptions fenced as `> **Figure (p.N):** …`.

Two honest caveats to plan around before implementation:

- **Vision-LLM consistency at 580 pages.** Table reconstruction and figure descriptions will vary in quality page to page, and tables are where hallucination risk concentrates. A verification pass is worth building in — page-count reconciliation plus spot-checking flagged pages (tables, dense figures).
- **Cross-page table merging needs page-boundary awareness.** Doable by carrying a short tail-context between consecutive pages or a post-pass that stitches a trailing headerless table onto the next page's leading table, then validating those specific spots.

Tell me the format your pipeline expects (or confirm my default), and whether the vision model should be a local one or an API — that's the last fork before I'd write anything.

Page boundaries are unimportant, as is front matter. Heading hierarchy is important, as is table format. I would imagine `**Figure**` should work as long as we're consistent. I also want this to be reusable, so I can replace these folders with some from other books and the process will pick them up and process them.

Design's locked enough to state back cleanly. Here's what the process does:

- Preserve the book's **heading hierarchy** (H1/H2/H3) as the model reads it off the page styling.
- Emit **GitHub-flavored Markdown tables**, with cross-page tables stitched into one.
- Mark figures/diagrams consistently as `**Figure**` with the generated description.
- Skip page markers and front matter entirely.
- One `<BookName>.md` per folder.

How the reusability works

- The script scans `book-scan/` for subfolders — **each subfolder is one book**. Folder name becomes the book title / H1.
- Pages are ordered deterministically by filename (your `ebook-page-0001.jpg` pattern sorts correctly).
- Drop in new book folders, rerun, and it processes whatever's there. No code changes per book.
- **Idempotent + resumable** (important at 580+ pages): it checkpoints per page and per book, so a crash or a new folder doesn't reprocess what's already done, and a failed run resumes instead of restarting. This also controls cost if we go the API route.
- Config lives in one place (model choice, input/output dirs), and it runs as a containerized CLI — openSUSE BCI base per your policy, which I'll justify concretely when building.

Two honest caveats, both tied to things you marked important

- **Heading hierarchy is the least reliable requirement.** The model infers H1/H2/H3 from visual styling (size, weight, position), and levels can drift across a long book — a section header occasionally read as a chapter title, or vice versa. A normalization post-pass reduces this but won't be perfect. Worth a spot-check step.
- **Reusability across very different books stresses the prompt.** A prose-heavy title and a figure-dense one need the same prompt to generalize. It will, but per-book quality varies, and heavy-diagram books lean harder on the figure-description step.

Last fork before implementation — two decisions the whole build hinges on:

content.text:
Your run failed for reasons that are fixable, and none of them mean local is the wrong call. Bottom line: the problem was mostly architecture plus likely the wrong model class, not your hardware. 32GB VRAM is plenty for this.

What almost certainly went wrong

- **The model probably couldn't see the image.** A tag like `qwen3.6:35b` reads as a **text-only** Qwen (and Qwen3 MoE variants are text-only). A text model can't transcribe a page — so the Hermes agent fell back to embedding the page as an image reference. That matches your symptom exactly: "majority of text rendered as an image." You need a true **vision-language model** (VLM), not a text MoE. (Flagging this as my leading diagnosis, not certainty — I didn't see your exact model tag or config.)
- **You ran the whole book through one growing context.** That's the token-space blowout. High-resolution document pages in Qwen-VL also expand into thousands of vision tokens each, so accumulating many pages in one agent conversation overflows fast.
- **An agent framework is the wrong tool here.** This is batch transcription, not an agentic task. Hermes adds tool-calling machinery and a shared context that caused both failure modes.

The architectural fix (this is 80% of the win)

- **One page per call, stateless.** Each image gets a single VLM inference with a fixed prompt; write that page's Markdown straight to disk; discard the context; next page. No conversation carryover, no whole-book accumulation. Memory and cost stay flat regardless of book length, and a 192-page book can't blow the context.
- **Plain script against your Ollama OpenAI-compatible endpoint** — no agent loop.
- **Cross-page table merge happens in a cheap post-pass**, after extraction, so the model stays stateless: detect a table at the bottom of page N and a headerless table at the top of page N+1, concatenate the rows.
- **Bound the input resolution.** Set Qwen-VL's max-pixels so each page maps to a predictable vision-token count, and give each page call a ~2–3k output-token budget (enough for a full page of Markdown). Your 1436×1527 scans are well within a sane cap.

Model options that fit 32GB (current landscape, just verified)

| Model | ~VRAM | Speed | Fit for this job |
|---|---|---|---|
| **qwen2.5vl:7b** | ~10–16GB | Fast | **Recommended default** — explicitly tuned for chart/document/layout, native Markdown + table output |
| **qwen3-vl:30b** (MoE, ~3B active) | ~20–24GB (quant) | Moderate | High-accuracy fallback for figure/table-heavy books; MoE keeps it feasible in your VRAM |
| **minicpm-v 4.5 (8B)** | ~10–14GB | Fast | Strong document-processing alternative |
| qwen3-vl:32b (dense) | ~24GB+ | ~400s/page | Avoid — too slow, skips pages |

VRAM figures are approximate and depend on quantization and your vision-token cap — treat them as ballpark.

My recommendation: start on **qwen2.5vl:7b** with the stateless per-page architecture. That combination directly fixes both of your failures and is the documented sweet spot for document/table OCR at your VRAM. Keep **qwen3-vl:30b** configured as an optional higher-accuracy pass for books where the 7B struggles on dense tables or figure descriptions — which slots naturally into the "skip if output exists" rerun model, since you can force-reprocess just the weak books on the bigger model.

One thing worth a quick real test before committing: run 3–5 representative pages (one plain-prose, one table, one figure-heavy) through qwen2.5vl:7b with the single-page prompt, and eyeball the Markdown. That tells us whether 7B is enough or whether the figure/table books need the 30B pass — cheaper to learn now than after building the full batch.

Want me to draft that small 3–5 page test harness first so we validate model quality before building the reusable pipeline? That's the lowest-risk next step, and it stays inside "don't build the whole thing yet."

Sources: [Ollama qwen2.5vl](https://ollama.com/library/qwen2.5vl), [Ollama qwen3-vl](https://ollama.com/library/qwen3-vl), [Local Vision-Language OCR Benchmark — nullmirror](https://nullmirror.com/en/blog/2026-05-24-local-vision-language-ocr-benchmark/)
Speed isn't an issue. I can just let it run. I'm going for qwen3-vl:30b to get the best accuracy

Locked. Here's the complete design in one place:

- **Input:** `book-scan/` subfolders, one book each, pages ordered by filename.
- **Model:** `qwen3-vl:30b` (MoE) local via Ollama, one page per call, **stateless** — write each page's Markdown to disk, discard context, next page.
- **Output:** one `<BookName>.md` per folder. No page markers, no front matter.
- **Structure:** preserved heading hierarchy (`#`/`##`/`###`), GFM tables, figures marked `**Figure**` with generated descriptions.
- **Cross-page tables:** stitched in a post-pass after extraction.
- **Reusability:** auto-discovers folders; **skip if output exists** (with `--force` to reprocess a specific book).

- **Verification pass:** page-count reconciliation + spot-check of table/figure-heavy pages.

Two config details specific to your qwen3-vl:30b choice — both tie directly to your earlier failure, so worth setting deliberately:

- **Set `num_ctx` high enough per call.** Ollama's default context is small (often 4096), and a dense page's vision tokens plus Markdown output can exceed that and truncate — a likely contributor to your "ran out of token space." I'd set it to ~12–16k per page call and cap output around 3k.
- **Watch VRAM headroom on the first few pages.** 30B-MoE weights (Q4) land near ~18–20GB, and high-resolution vision tokens consume KV/activation memory on top. On 32GB it fits, but bound `max-pixels` so a full-res page doesn't push you into OOM. If it's tight on the first book, lowering max-pixels is the first lever. (VRAM figures are approximate — depends on the exact quant Ollama pulls.)

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
ocr_books.py - Turn folders of scanned book pages into per-book Markdown.

Each subfolder of the input directory is treated as one book. Every page image
is sent, one at a time and statelessly, to a local vision-language model served
by Ollama. The model transcribes the page into Markdown, preserving heading
hierarchy and tables, and describing figures/diagrams as `**Figure:** ...`.
Per-page output is cached so a long run is fully resumable; the cached pages are
then assembled (with cross-page tables stitched and running headers/footers
stripped) into one `<Book Name>.md` per folder.

Design notes
------------
* One page = one model call, no conversation carryover. Memory and context stay
  flat regardless of book length, which is what keeps a 200-page book from
  overrunning the context window.
* The Ollama *native* API (/api/chat) is used rather than the OpenAI-compat
  shim so num_ctx and other generation options can be set reliably per call.
* Resumability: each page is cached under <cache>/<book>/page-XXXX.md. Re-running
  skips pages already cached and books whose final .md already exists.

Author-selected defaults (tunable via flags):
    num_ctx      = 16384   # room for ~2.7k vision tokens + prompt + output
    num_predict  = 4096    # max Markdown tokens per page (dense page headroom)
    max_pixels   = 2_600_000  # guard; the 1436x1527 scans pass through untouched
    temperature  = 0       # deterministic transcription
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import base64
import io
import json
import logging
import os
import re
import sys
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional
from urllib import request as urlrequest
from urllib.error import URLError, HTTPError

try:
    from PIL import Image
except ImportError:
    sys.exit("Pillow is required. Install with: pip install pillow requests")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Configuration
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

IMAGE_EXTS = {".jpg", ".jpeg", ".png", ".tif", ".tiff", ".webp", ".bmp"}
BLANK_SENTINEL = "<<BLANK>>"
PAGE_BREAK = "\n<<<PAGE_BREAK>>>\n"

DEFAULTS = {
    "model": "qwen3-vl:30b",
    "host": os.environ.get("OLLAMA_HOST", "http://localhost:11434"),
    "num_ctx": 16384,
    "num_predict": 4096,
    "max_pixels": 2_600_000,
    "temperature": 0.0,
    "timeout": 1800,      # seconds per page request (speed is not a concern)
    "retries": 3,
}

PROMPT = """You are transcribing a single scanned page from a printed book into clean Markdown.

Rules:
- Transcribe ALL body text exactly, preserving natural reading order.
- Represent the page's visual heading hierarchy with Markdown headings: use # for a chapter or major title, ## for a section heading, ### for a subsection. Judge levels from the relative size and prominence of the text.
- Render every table as a GitHub-Flavored Markdown table with a header row and separator. Keep columns aligned to the source. If a table clearly continues from a previous page (no repeated header), still output the rows as a GFM table.
- For any photograph, illustration, chart, or diagram, DO NOT embed the image. Instead insert a single line: **Figure:** followed by a concise, factual description of what the figure shows (include any visible caption text).
- Do NOT transcribe running headers, running footers, or standalone page numbers.
- Do NOT add commentary, notes, or explanations of your own. Output only the page's Markdown.
- Do NOT wrap your entire answer in a code fence.
- If the page has no readable content (blank or effectively empty), output exactly: {blank}

Transcribe the page now.""".format(blank=BLANK_SENTINEL)


@dataclass
class Config:
    input_dir: Path
    output_dir: Path
    cache_dir: Path
    model: str
    host: str
    num_ctx: int
    num_predict: int
    max_pixels: int
    temperature: float
    timeout: int
    retries: int
    force: bool
    only_book: Optional[str]
    merge_tables: bool
    strip_boilerplate: bool
    dry_run: bool


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Discovery
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def discover_books(input_dir: Path) -> list[tuple[str, list[Path]]]:
    """Return [(book_name, [sorted page image paths]), ...] for each subfolder."""
    books: list[tuple[str, list[Path]]] = []
    for entry in sorted(input_dir.iterdir(), key=lambda p: p.name.lower()):
        if not entry.is_dir():
            continue
        if entry.name.startswith(".") or entry.name.startswith("_"):
            continue  # skip hidden and our own _output/_cache dirs
        pages = sorted(
            (p for p in entry.iterdir() if p.suffix.lower() in IMAGE_EXTS),
            key=lambda p: p.name.lower(),
        )
        if pages:
            books.append((entry.name, pages))
    return books


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Image encoding
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def encode_image(path: Path, max_pixels: int) -> str:
    """Load an image, downscale only if it exceeds max_pixels, return base64 JPEG."""
    with Image.open(path) as im:
        im = im.convert("RGB")
        w, h = im.size
        if w * h > max_pixels:
            scale = (max_pixels / (w * h)) ** 0.5
            im = im.resize((max(1, int(w * scale)), max(1, int(h * scale))), Image.LANCZOS)
        buf = io.BytesIO()
        im.save(buf, format="JPEG", quality=92)
        return base64.b64encode(buf.getvalue()).decode("ascii")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Ollama call
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def ocr_page(cfg: Config, image_b64: str) -> str:
    """Send one page to Ollama /api/chat and return the raw Markdown string."""
    payload = {
        "model": cfg.model,
        "stream": False,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT, "images": [image_b64]}],
        "options": {
            "num_ctx": cfg.num_ctx,
            "num_predict": cfg.num_predict,
            "temperature": cfg.temperature,
        },
    }
    data = json.dumps(payload).encode("utf-8")
    url = cfg.host.rstrip("/") + "/api/chat"

    last_err: Optional[Exception] = None
    for attempt in range(1, cfg.retries + 1):
        try:
            req = urlrequest.Request(url, data=data, headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"})
            with urlrequest.urlopen(req, timeout=cfg.timeout) as resp:
                body = json.loads(resp.read().decode("utf-8"))
            return body["message"]["content"]
        except (URLError, HTTPError, KeyError, TimeoutError, json.JSONDecodeError) as e:
            last_err = e
            wait = min(30, 2 ** attempt)
            logging.warning("  page call failed (attempt %d/%d): %s - retrying in %ds",
                            attempt, cfg.retries, e, wait)
            time.sleep(wait)
    raise RuntimeError(f"Ollama call failed after {cfg.retries} attempts: {last_err}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Output cleanup
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def unwrap_code_fence(text: str) -> str:
    """Strip a fence that wraps the ENTIRE answer (```markdown ... ```)."""
    t = text.strip()
    if t.startswith("```"):
        lines = t.split("\n")
        if lines[-1].strip().startswith("```"):
            return "\n".join(lines[1:-1]).strip()
    return t


def is_table_line(line: str) -> bool:
    return line.lstrip().startswith("|")


def is_separator_line(line: str) -> bool:
    s = line.strip()
    return bool(re.match(r"^\|?[\s:|-]+\|?$", s)) and "-" in s


def strip_running_headers(pages: list[str], num_pages: int) -> tuple[list[str], list[str]]:
    """Remove short lines that repeat across many pages (running heads/feet).

    A line is treated as boilerplate if a normalized copy of it appears as the
    first or last non-empty line on at least threshold pages. Pure page-number
    lines are always removed.
    """
    threshold = max(10, int(0.4 * num_pages))
    edge_counts: dict[str, int] = {}

    def norm(s: str) -> str:
        return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s.strip()).lower()

    for page in pages:
        lines = [l for l in page.split("\n") if l.strip()]
        if not lines:
            continue
        for edge in {lines[0], lines[-1]}:
            if len(edge) < 80:
                edge_counts[norm(edge)] = edge_counts.get(norm(edge), 0) + 1

    boilerplate = {k for k, v in edge_counts.items() if v >= threshold and k}
    removed_log = sorted(boilerplate)

    pagenum_re = re.compile(r"^\s*(?:page\s+)?[ivxlcdm\d]{1,6}\s*$", re.IGNORECASE)

    cleaned: list[str] = []
    for page in pages:
        out_lines = []
        for line in page.split("\n"):
            n = norm(line)
            if n in boilerplate:
                continue
            if line.strip() and pagenum_re.match(line.strip()) and not is_table_line(line):
                continue
            out_lines.append(line)
        cleaned.append("\n".join(out_lines).strip("\n"))
    return cleaned, removed_log


def merge_cross_page_tables(pages: list[str]) -> tuple[str, int]:
    """Assemble pages, stitching a table that continues onto the next page.

    Conservative: only merges when the previous page ends with a table row and
    the next page begins with a table row of the SAME column count. The
    continuation table's header+separator (if present) is dropped and its body
    rows are appended to the previous table.
    """
    merged: list[str] = []
    merge_count = 0

    for page in pages:
        page = page.strip("\n")
        if not page:
            continue
        if not merged:
            merged.append(page)
            continue

        prev_lines = merged[-1].split("\n")
        cur_lines = page.split("\n")

        # last non-empty line of previous page
        i = len(prev_lines) - 1
        while i >= 0 and not prev_lines[i].strip():
            i -= 1
        # first non-empty line of current page
        j = 0
        while j < len(cur_lines) and not cur_lines[j].strip():
            j += 1

        if i >= 0 and j < len(cur_lines) and is_table_line(prev_lines[i]) and is_table_line(cur_lines[j]):
            if prev_lines[i].count("|") == cur_lines[j].count("|"):
                # span of the leading table block on the current page
                m = j
                lead_idx = []
                while m < len(cur_lines) and is_table_line(cur_lines[m]):
                    lead_idx.append(m)
                    m += 1
                drop = 2 if len(lead_idx) >= 2 and is_separator_line(cur_lines[lead_idx[1]]) else 0
                body = [cur_lines[x] for x in lead_idx[drop:]]
                rest = "\n".join(cur_lines[m:]).strip("\n")

                merged[-1] = "\n".join(prev_lines[: i + 1] + body)
                if rest:
                    merged.append(rest)
                merge_count += 1
                continue

        merged.append(page)

    return "\n\n".join(merged), merge_count


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Per-book processing
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class BookStats:
    name: str = ""
    total_pages: int = 0
    blank_pages: int = 0
    tables_merged: int = 0
    boilerplate_removed: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    seconds: float = 0.0
    output_path: str = ""


def process_book(cfg: Config, name: str, pages: list[Path]) -> BookStats:
    stats = BookStats(name=name, total_pages=len(pages))
    out_path = cfg.output_dir / f"{name}.md"

    if out_path.exists() and not cfg.force:
        logging.info("[%s] output exists - skipping (use --force to rebuild)", name)
        stats.output_path = str(out_path)
        stats.seconds = -1.0  # sentinel: skipped
        return stats

    book_cache = cfg.cache_dir / name
    book_cache.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    start = time.time()
    page_markdowns: list[str] = []

    for idx, page_path in enumerate(pages, start=1):
        cache_file = book_cache / f"page-{idx:04d}.md"
        if cache_file.exists() and not cfg.force:
            raw = cache_file.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
        else:
            logging.info("[%s] page %d/%d: %s", name, idx, len(pages), page_path.name)
            image_b64 = encode_image(page_path, cfg.max_pixels)
            raw = unwrap_code_fence(ocr_page(cfg, image_b64))
            cache_file.write_text(raw, encoding="utf-8")

        if raw.strip() == BLANK_SENTINEL or not raw.strip():
            stats.blank_pages += 1
            page_markdowns.append("")
        else:
            page_markdowns.append(raw)

    # ---- assemble ----
    non_blank = [p for p in page_markdowns if p.strip()]
    if cfg.strip_boilerplate:
        non_blank, removed = strip_running_headers(non_blank, len(pages))
        stats.boilerplate_removed = removed

    if cfg.merge_tables:
        document, merged = merge_cross_page_tables(non_blank)
        stats.tables_merged = merged
    else:
        document = "\n\n".join(p.strip("\n") for p in non_blank if p.strip())

    document = re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", document).strip() + "\n"

    cfg.output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    out_path.write_text(document, encoding="utf-8")
    stats.output_path = str(out_path)
    stats.seconds = time.time() - start
    return stats


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# CLI
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def build_config(args: argparse.Namespace) -> Config:
    input_dir = Path(args.input).expanduser().resolve()
    output_dir = Path(args.output).expanduser().resolve() if args.output else input_dir / "_output"
    cache_dir = Path(args.cache).expanduser().resolve() if args.cache else input_dir / "_cache"
    return Config(
        input_dir=input_dir,
        output_dir=output_dir,
        cache_dir=cache_dir,
        model=args.model,
        host=args.host,
        num_ctx=args.num_ctx,
        num_predict=args.num_predict,
        max_pixels=args.max_pixels,
        temperature=args.temperature,
        timeout=args.timeout,
        retries=args.retries,
        force=args.force,
        only_book=args.book,
        merge_tables=not args.no_merge_tables,
        strip_boilerplate=not args.keep_running_headers,
        dry_run=args.dry_run,
    )


def main(argv: Optional[list[str]] = None) -> int:
    p = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="OCR scanned book folders into per-book Markdown via a local Ollama VLM.")
    p.add_argument("--input", default=str(Path(__file__).resolve().parent),
                   help="Directory whose subfolders are books (default: this script's folder).")
    p.add_argument("--output", default=None, help="Where to write <Book>.md (default: <input>/_output).")
    p.add_argument("--cache", default=None, help="Per-page cache dir (default: <input>/_cache).")
    p.add_argument("--model", default=DEFAULTS["model"], help="Ollama model tag (e.g. qwen3-vl:30b or qwen2.5vl:7b).")
    p.add_argument("--host", default=DEFAULTS["host"], help="Ollama host URL.")
    p.add_argument("--num-ctx", type=int, default=DEFAULTS["num_ctx"], dest="num_ctx")
    p.add_argument("--num-predict", type=int, default=DEFAULTS["num_predict"], dest="num_predict")
    p.add_argument("--max-pixels", type=int, default=DEFAULTS["max_pixels"], dest="max_pixels")
    p.add_argument("--temperature", type=float, default=DEFAULTS["temperature"])
    p.add_argument("--timeout", type=int, default=DEFAULTS["timeout"], help="Per-page request timeout (s).")
    p.add_argument("--retries", type=int, default=DEFAULTS["retries"])
    p.add_argument("--book", default=None, help="Process only the book whose folder name matches this.")
    p.add_argument("--force", action="store_true", help="Reprocess even if output/cache already exist.")
    p.add_argument("--no-merge-tables", action="store_true", help="Disable cross-page table stitching.")
    p.add_argument("--keep-running-headers", action="store_true", help="Do not strip repeated headers/footers.")
    p.add_argument("--dry-run", action="store_true", help="List discovered books and page counts, then exit.")
    args = p.parse_args(argv)

    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(message)s")
    cfg = build_config(args)

    if not cfg.input_dir.is_dir():
        logging.error("Input directory not found: %s", cfg.input_dir)
        return 2

    books = discover_books(cfg.input_dir)
    if cfg.only_book:
        books = [b for b in books if b[0] == cfg.only_book]
        if not books:
            logging.error("No book folder named %r under %s", cfg.only_book, cfg.input_dir)
            return 2

    if not books:
        logging.error("No book subfolders with images found under %s", cfg.input_dir)
        return 2

    logging.info("Discovered %d book(s) under %s:", len(books), cfg.input_dir)
    for name, pages in books:
        logging.info("  - %-45s %4d pages", name, len(pages))

    if cfg.dry_run:
        logging.info("Dry run - no model calls made.")
        return 0

    logging.info("Model: %s via %s  (num_ctx=%d, num_predict=%d, max_pixels=%d)",
                 cfg.model, cfg.host, cfg.num_ctx, cfg.num_predict, cfg.max_pixels)

    all_stats: list[BookStats] = []
    for name, pages in books:
        try:
            all_stats.append(process_book(cfg, name, pages))
        except Exception as e:  # keep going to the next book
            logging.error("[%s] FAILED: %s", name, e)

    # ---- verification summary ----
    logging.info("\n===== SUMMARY =====")
    for s in all_stats:
        if s.seconds == -1.0:
            logging.info("%-45s SKIPPED (output existed)", s.name)
            continue
        accounted = s.blank_pages  # blanks + non-blank should equal total
        logging.info("%-45s pages=%d blank=%d tables_merged=%d boilerplate_lines=%d  %.0fs",
                     s.name, s.total_pages, s.blank_pages, s.tables_merged,
                     len(s.boilerplate_removed), s.seconds)
        if s.boilerplate_removed:
            for line in s.boilerplate_removed:
                logging.info("      stripped repeated line: %r", line)
        logging.info("      -> %s", s.output_path)
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

# Justification: this job needs only CPython plus Pillow (all HTTP is stdlib

# No GPU libraries are needed here - the model runs in your Ollama server; this

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

input.content:
# book-scan → Markdown

Turns each subfolder of scanned book pages into a single Markdown file, using a
local vision-language model served by Ollama. Built for feeding Karakeep (and
whatever downstream pipeline ingests from there into Milvus).

## What it does

- Treats **each subfolder as one book**; pages are ordered by filename.
- Sends **one page at a time, statelessly** to the model — no growing context,
  so book length never overruns the context window.
- Preserves **heading hierarchy** (`#`/`##`/`###`) and renders **GFM tables**.
- Describes photos, charts, and diagrams as `**Figure:** …` instead of embedding
  images.
- **Stitches tables that span two pages** and strips repeated running
  headers/footers and bare page numbers.
- **Resumable**: every page is cached, so an interrupted run picks up where it
  left off. Books whose output already exists are **skipped** (rerun-friendly —
  drop in new folders and run again).

Output: `_output/<Book Name>.md` (one file per folder). Cache: `_cache/`.

## Setup

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt          # just Pillow; HTTP is stdlib
ollama pull qwen3-vl:30b                  # primary model
ollama pull qwen2.5vl:7b                  # optional faster fallback
```

## Run

From inside the `book-scan` folder:

```bash
# See what it will process, no model calls:
python ocr_books.py --dry-run

# Process everything with the default model (qwen3-vl:30b):
python ocr_books.py

# Point at a remote Ollama in your lab:
python ocr_books.py --host http://labserver:11434

# Switch to the fallback model:
python ocr_books.py --model qwen2.5vl:7b

# Rebuild a single book from scratch:
python ocr_books.py --book "A Whole New Mind" --force
```

## Key settings (chosen defaults)

| Flag | Default | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `--num-ctx` | 16384 | Fits ~2.7k vision tokens for a full-res page + prompt + output |
| `--num-predict` | 4096 | Headroom for a dense page of Markdown |
| `--max-pixels` | 2600000 | Guard for oversized scans; your 1436×1527 pages pass through untouched |
| `--temperature` | 0 | Deterministic transcription |
| `--timeout` | 1800 | Generous per-page wait; speed is not a concern |

Other flags: `--no-merge-tables`, `--keep-running-headers`, `--force`,
`--book NAME`, `--output DIR`, `--cache DIR`.

## Verification

After each run a summary prints per book: total pages, blank pages, tables
merged, and every repeated line it stripped (so you can confirm it only removed
boilerplate). Spot-check the table- and figure-heavy pages first — those are
where a VLM is most likely to slip.

## Container (optional)

```bash
docker build -t book-ocr .
docker run --rm -v "$PWD:/data" \
    -e OLLAMA_HOST=http://host.docker.internal:11434 book-ocr --input /data
```

Base image is openSUSE BCI Python per standing policy; the container is only the
HTTP client that drives your Ollama server.

## Notes / known limits

- **Heading levels** are inferred from visual styling and can occasionally drift
  (a section read as a chapter). This is the least reliable requirement — worth
  a spot-check.
- **Cross-page table merging** is conservative: it only joins when the last line
  of a page and the first line of the next are both table rows with matching
  column counts. Unusual splits may still need a manual touch-up.
- The model choice, prompt, and all thresholds live at the top of
  `ocr_books.py` if you want to tune them.

# --- cross-page table merge ---

p2 = "| x | y | z |\n| - | - | - |\n| 7 | 8 | 9 |"

assert n2 == 0, f"mismatched cols should not merge, got {n2}"

print(f"PASS boilerplate: stripped {removed}")

assert o.unwrap_code_fence("# No fence\n\ntext") == "# No fence\n\ntext"
print("PASS fence unwrap")

# --- blank sentinel handling is via constant ---

print("PASS constants")
print("\nALL SELF-TESTS PASSED")

sidesteps all of this — the container is optional here.
